text:
MULTIDISCIPLINARY PANCREATIC ONCOLOGY TUMOR BOARD CONSULTATION NOTE
Medical Oncology / Hepatobiliary Surgery / Radiation Oncology

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT

Ms. Margaret Osei-Bonsu is a 66-year-old woman of Ghanaian descent, retired librarian, presenting to the multidisciplinary pancreatic tumor board for treatment planning following new diagnosis of borderline resectable pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma of the pancreatic head and uncinate process.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

Ms. Osei-Bonsu presented to her primary care physician approximately 10 weeks ago with a constellation of symptoms that prompted urgent specialist referral. She described a 10-week progressive history of painless jaundice, associated with significant pruritus, dark urine, and pale, greasy stools consistent with obstructive jaundice and exocrine insufficiency. She also endorsed approximately 14 pounds of unintentional weight loss over this period, which she attributed initially to reduced appetite related to epigastric discomfort. She described a dull, gnawing epigastric pain that radiates into the mid-back, worse after meals. She denies any prior history of pancreatitis, pancreatic cysts, or prior abdominal malignancy.

On examination at the time of initial presentation, she was noted to have scleral icterus, mild hepatomegaly, and a palpable, non-tender gallbladder (a Courvoisier sign), raising immediate suspicion for malignant biliary obstruction from a pancreatic head lesion. Laboratory evaluation was notable for total bilirubin 8.2 mg/dL (direct 6.9 mg/dL), alkaline phosphatase 420 U/L, AST 98 U/L, ALT 112 U/L, albumin 3.1 g/dL, CA 19-9 markedly elevated at 1,204 U/mL, and CEA mildly elevated at 7.1 ng/mL.

Subsequent pancreatic protocol CT demonstrated a 3.8 cm hypoenhancing mass in the pancreatic head and uncinate process with upstream main pancreatic duct dilation to 7 mm and common bile duct dilation to 16 mm. The mass was found to abut the superior mesenteric artery (SMA) over approximately 180 degrees of its circumference and involved the superior mesenteric vein (SMV) with focal luminal narrowing but without complete occlusion. No hepatic lesions or peritoneal implants were identified. Two sub-centimeter peripancreatic lymph nodes were noted (up to 0.9 cm). MRI abdomen with MRCP corroborated these findings, confirming SMA contact of approximately 170 degrees and SMV narrowing without occlusion, with a patent main portal vein. No hepatic metastases were identified on MRI.

Endoscopic ultrasound (EUS)-guided fine needle biopsy of the pancreatic head mass was performed. Pathology demonstrated moderately differentiated pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma (PDAC). Immunohistochemistry was positive for CK7, CK19, MUC1, and CA 19-9, and negative for CK20 and CDX2, confirming pancreatobiliary origin and excluding metastatic colorectal adenocarcinoma, acinar cell carcinoma, and neuroendocrine tumor. Ki-67 proliferation index was approximately 35%, consistent with moderate proliferative activity. Lymphovascular invasion was not identified on the biopsy specimen.

The case was referred to our multidisciplinary pancreatic tumor board for consensus treatment planning. This note represents the medical oncology consultation from that tumor board discussion.

---

PAST MEDICAL HISTORY

1. Type 2 diabetes mellitus — currently diet-controlled; no insulin or oral hypoglycemics at baseline
2. Hypertension — on amlodipine
3. Obstructive jaundice secondary to pancreatic head adenocarcinoma — as above
4. No prior abdominal surgeries
5. No prior history of pancreatitis or pancreatic cysts
6. No prior malignancy

---

SOCIAL HISTORY

Ms. Osei-Bonsu is a retired librarian of Ghanaian descent. She is widowed and lives independently. She reports a 15-pack-year smoking history and quit approximately 10 years ago. She denies current alcohol use. She lives with her adult daughter nearby, who accompanies her to appointments and will be a key support person during treatment. She is highly motivated and engaged in her care, having researched her diagnosis extensively prior to today's visit.

---

FAMILY HISTORY

No known family history of pancreatic cancer. No family history of breast, ovarian, colon, or other gastrointestinal malignancies. Mother died of cardiovascular disease in her 80s. Father died of stroke. Two siblings alive and well. Germline testing will be recommended given the diagnosis of PDAC; however, family history is not suggestive of a hereditary syndrome at this time.

---

ALLERGIES

No known drug allergies.

---

MEDICATIONS

1. Amlodipine 5 mg orally once daily
2. Ondansetron 8 mg orally as needed for nausea (recent prescription from PCP)
3. Prochlorperazine 10 mg orally as needed for breakthrough nausea
4. No anticoagulation
5. No antiplatelet agents
6. No metformin or insulin (diabetes diet-controlled)

---

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS

Positive for: progressive jaundice, pruritus, dark urine, pale/greasy stools, 14-pound unintentional weight loss, epigastric pain radiating to the back, fatigue, reduced appetite, and early satiety.

Negative for: fever, chills, hematemesis, melena, hematochezia, chest pain, dyspnea, lower extremity edema, new neurological symptoms, skin rash, or joint pain.

---

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION

Vital signs: Blood pressure 128/76 mmHg, heart rate 82 bpm, respiratory rate 16/min, temperature 98.4°F, SpO2 98% on room air, weight 148 lbs (BMI 24.5, down from an estimated baseline of ~162 lbs per PCP records).

General: Alert, oriented, well-groomed woman in no acute distress. Mild icteric appearance of skin. Visibly fatigued but conversational and engaged.

HEENT: Scleral icterus present bilaterally. Oropharynx clear. No lymphadenopathy in cervical or supraclavicular chains.

Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm. No murmurs, rubs, or gallops. No jugular venous distension.

Respiratory: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. No wheezes or rhonchi.

Abdomen: Soft, mildly tender to deep palpation in the epigastric and right upper quadrant regions. A palpable, non-tender gallbladder is noted beneath the right costal margin (consistent with Courvoisier sign, already documented). No peritoneal signs. Mild hepatomegaly on percussion. Bowel sounds are present and active. No ascites on clinical exam.

Extremities: No peripheral edema. No palpable masses.

Skin: Mild jaundice. No scratch marks from pruritus noted on exam. No spider angiomata or palmar erythema.

Neurological: Grossly intact. No focal deficits.

---

LABORATORY RESULTS

Complete blood count: WBC 7.2 x10^9/L, hemoglobin 11.8 g/dL (mild anemia of chronic disease), platelets 264 x10^9/L.

Comprehensive metabolic panel: Total bilirubin 8.2 mg/dL, direct bilirubin 6.9 mg/dL, alkaline phosphatase 420 U/L, AST 98 U/L, ALT 112 U/L, GGT 318 U/L, albumin 3.1 g/dL, total protein 6.4 g/dL, creatinine 0.82 mg/dL, BUN 14 mg/dL, glucose 118 mg/dL (mildly elevated, consistent with known diabetes and stress response).

Coagulation: PT/INR 1.1. PTT within normal limits.

Tumor markers: CA 19-9 1,204 U/mL (markedly elevated; upper limit of normal 37 U/mL), CEA 7.1 ng/mL (mildly elevated).

Note: The patient has no known Gilbert syndrome; Lewis antigen status has not been formally assessed, though very high CA 19-9 in this clinical context is highly consistent with malignancy rather than a false-positive elevation.

---

IMAGING RESULTS

Pancreatic protocol CT abdomen/pelvis (reviewed at tumor board): 3.8 cm hypoenhancing mass in the pancreatic head and uncinate process. Main pancreatic duct dilation to 7 mm upstream. Common bile duct dilated to 16 mm with abrupt cutoff at the level of the pancreatic head, consistent with malignant obstruction. The mass abuts the SMA over approximately 180 degrees of its circumference and involves the SMV with focal narrowing. No encasement of the celiac axis or proper hepatic artery. No hepatic lesions. No peritoneal implants. Two peripancreatic lymph nodes measuring up to 0.9 cm — suspicious for nodal involvement given size and location, though formally sub-centimeter. Classified as borderline resectable by AHPBA/SSO/SSAT criteria based on SMA abutment exceeding 180 degrees and SMV involvement.

MRI abdomen with MRCP: Corroborates the CT findings. SMA contact estimated at approximately 170 degrees. SMV narrowing present but without complete occlusion; main portal vein is patent. No hepatic lesions. No ascites. Main pancreatic duct 6.5 mm in the body proximal to the obstruction. No distant metastatic disease identified.

---

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN

Ms. Osei-Bonsu is a 66-year-old woman with a new diagnosis of borderline resectable pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma of the pancreatic head and uncinate process, clinical stage T3 N1 M0 (AJCC 8th edition, Stage IIB). Her disease is classified as borderline resectable based on SMA abutment of approximately 170–180 degrees and SMV involvement with focal luminal narrowing, per AHPBA/SSO/SSAT criteria. She has biopsy-proven moderately differentiated PDAC with CK7/CK19/MUC1-positive immunoprofile. Her CA 19-9 is markedly elevated at 1,204 U/mL. Performance status is ECOG 1. She currently has significant biliary obstruction with bilirubin 8.2 mg/dL that will need to be addressed prior to systemic chemotherapy.

The case was discussed in detail at our multidisciplinary pancreatic tumor board today, attended by hepatobiliary surgery, medical oncology, radiation oncology, interventional gastroenterology (endoscopy), and diagnostic radiology. The following consensus recommendations were made:

1. BILIARY DECOMPRESSION: The patient will be referred urgently to interventional gastroenterology for ERCP with placement of a fully covered self-expanding metallic biliary stent (FCSEMS). Stent placement is recommended over plastic stents for durability, given that the anticipated neoadjuvant course will be several months in duration. Biliary decompression is necessary to safely administer chemotherapy — the current bilirubin of 8.2 mg/dL would preclude safe dosing of Camptosar and impair clearance of multiple chemotherapy agents. We expect bilirubin to normalize within 2–3 weeks following successful stent placement. Repeat LFTs will be checked at 2 weeks post-ERCP before chemotherapy initiation.

2. NEOADJUVANT CHEMOTHERAPY WITH MODIFIED FOLFIRINOX: Following biliary decompression and normalization of liver function tests, the patient will initiate neoadjuvant chemotherapy with a modified FOLFIRINOX regimen. This will consist of oxali 85 mg/m2, leucovorin 400 mg/m2, iri 150 mg/m2 (reduced from the standard 180 mg/m2 given the presence of a biliary stent and a mild concern regarding Gilbert syndrome-like physiology given her mildly elevated bilirubin history — we will reassess Camptosar dosing after cycle 1 based on LFT normalization), and Adrucil 2400 mg/m2 as a 46-hour continuous infusion, administered every 14 days. Treatment intent is curative/neoadjuvant. Eight cycles (approximately 16 weeks) are planned. FOLFIRINOX was selected over gem/nab-pac based on her relatively young age, good performance status (ECOG 1), and the data from the PRODIGE 4/ACCORD 11 trial and subsequent neoadjuvant PDAC literature supporting superior tumor downstaging with FOLFIRINOX in fit patients with borderline resectable disease.

   Granulocyte colony-stimulating factor (G-CSF) support with Neulasta (pegfilgrastim) will be administered with each cycle of FOLFIRINOX given the known risk of febrile neutropenia with this regimen.

   The goals of neoadjuvant therapy include: (a) potential downstaging of the SMA abutment to less than 90 degrees to allow safer margin-negative resection; (b) early treatment of potential micrometastatic disease; (c) use of the neoadjuvant period as a biological test — patients who develop disease progression during neoadjuvant therapy are spared the morbidity of a Whipple procedure that would not have been beneficial.

3. RESTAGING: Restaging CT with pancreatic protocol will be performed after cycles 4 and 8. CA 19-9 will be checked after every 2 cycles. If CA 19-9 fails to decline or imaging demonstrates disease progression, the treatment plan will be reassessed, including consideration of alternative chemotherapy regimens or palliative intent.

4. CONSIDERATION OF CHEMORADIATION AND SURGERY: Following completion of planned neoadjuvant chemotherapy, restaging will be performed. If the patient demonstrates continued disease control or response, we will reassess resectability. The tumor board has noted that consolidation chemoradiation (long-course with a radiosensitizing fluoropyrimidine, or SBRT) prior to surgical resection may be considered as an additional step to further sterilize the surgical margin, particularly the SMA margin, before Whipple procedure. Surgical eligibility will be formally reassessed at that time by hepatobiliary surgery.

5. GERMLINE TESTING: Germline genetic testing for hereditary pancreatic cancer syndromes (including BRCA1/2, PALB2, ATM, MLH1/MSH2/MSH6/PMS2/EPCAM, CDKN2A, and STK11) has been ordered today via a blood-based germline panel. Results are pending. Germline testing in all newly diagnosed PDAC patients is now standard per NCCP guidelines, and results could influence both family counseling and therapeutic eligibility (e.g., PARP inhibitor maintenance if a BRCA2 mutation is found).

6. NUTRITIONAL AND SUPPORTIVE CARE: The patient has lost 14 pounds and has an albumin of 3.1 g/dL, indicating significant nutritional compromise. Referral to oncology dietitian has been placed. She has exocrine insufficiency-like symptoms (greasy stools) secondary to pancreatic duct obstruction and may benefit from a trial of pancreatic enzyme replacement therapy (PERT) with meals. Palliative care has been co-introduced as a supportive care resource, which the patient accepted. Diabetes management will be monitored throughout chemotherapy given her diet-controlled type 2 diabetes and the potential for blood glucose fluctuations on a dexamethasone-containing antiemetic regimen.

7. PORT PLACEMENT: Given the planned 8 cycles of intravenous chemotherapy involving continuous 5-FU infusion via ambulatory pump, placement of a tunneled central venous access device (port-a-cath) is recommended. Referral to interventional radiology for port placement has been placed, to be performed after biliary stent placement and prior to cycle 1.

8. PATIENT COUNSELING: Extensive time was spent discussing the diagnosis, treatment rationale, goals of neoadjuvant therapy, and realistic expectations regarding resectability. The patient was counseled that even after an excellent response to chemotherapy, surgical resection is not guaranteed — it will depend on reassessment of vascular involvement. However, the patient expressed a very clear, strong desire to pursue aggressive curative-intent therapy. She was accompanied by her daughter and both asked thoughtful questions. Informed consent for neoadjuvant FOLFIRINOX was obtained and documented. The patient was given written patient education materials and scheduled for a return visit in 2 weeks following ERCP for chemotherapy planning.

Returning faculty: Medical Oncology (attending), Hepatobiliary Surgery (attending), Radiation Oncology (attending), Interventional Gastroenterology, Diagnostic Radiology.

Next appointment: Medical oncology follow-up in 2 weeks, post-ERCP LFTs to be reviewed prior to chemotherapy initiation.

Electronically signed by: Attending Medical Oncologist, Multidisciplinary Pancreatic Oncology Program.
